one_line_takeaway: AegisLLM shows that a 4-agent, prompt-optimized, inference-time defense can match training-based safety methods on jailbreak resistance and beat model-modification methods on WMDP/TOFU unlearning -- while retaining far more benign-query utility and requiring no weight retraining.
key_finding: Casting security as an inference-time multi-agent problem yields a favorable robustness-vs-utility tradeoff without retraining. On WMDP unlearning (Llama-3-8B, Table 2) AegisLLM reaches Cyber 24.4% / Bio 25.4% / Chem 27.2% -- the lowest hazardous-knowledge accuracy of all methods and near the 25% random-guess floor -- while retaining MMLU 58.4% and MT-Bench 7.57. On jailbreaking (Table 4) it does NOT achieve the single best StrongREJECT score; its 0.038 is competitive with but higher (worse) than Self-Reminders 0.015, LLM-LAT 0.021, and Circuit Breaker 0.022. The real win is the tradeoff: those stronger-resistance methods collapse benign-query utility (full-refusal 46-56%, compliance 32-49%), whereas AegisLLM keeps 88.5% compliance and only 7.9% false refusals, roughly matching Llama Guard's 0.039 resistance at far lower over-refusal. It also adapts fast (abstract: ~51% StrongREJECT reduction vs base 0.078->0.038; from just 20 training examples and <300 LM calls on WMDP).
core_contribution: AegisLLM is a cooperative, inference-time multi-agent defense for LLM security. The novel contribution is a 4-agent security architecture -- Orchestrator (routes queries by safety assessment), Deflector (issues refusals/sanitized responses for unsafe queries), Responder (answers benign queries with high utility), and Evaluator (post-hoc safety check that can loop the flow back) -- plus the broader paradigm of scaling security defenses at TEST/INFERENCE time rather than through training-time model modification. The system self-improves by automatically optimizing each agent's system prompt (instructions + in-context demonstrations) against a small labeled validation set, requiring no retraining of the backbone LLM. Note: the paper APPLIES existing tooling (DSPy modules/signatures, the MIPROv2 Bayesian prompt optimizer, and a Tree-structured Parzen Estimator) rather than inventing prompt optimization; the originality is in casting LLM security as a modular multi-agent, inference-time optimization problem.
threat_model: Two adversary classes, framed as the top-2 OWASP LLM risks (2024/2025): (1) Sensitive Information Disclosure -- a user attempts to extract hazardous or restricted knowledge the model was supposed to have 'unlearned' (WMDP weapons-of-mass-destruction proxy knowledge; TOFU fictional-author facts). (2) Prompt Injection / Jailbreaking -- adversarial prompts crafted to bypass safety guardrails and elicit harmful content (StrongREJECT). The defender controls the surrounding agentic system and a small pool of labeled examples but does NOT modify model weights; the backbone LLM still internally contains the hazardous knowledge, so the guarantee is only as strong as the Orchestrator/Evaluator agents' detection. A dual objective is explicit: block/deflect harmful queries while minimizing false refusals of benign-but-suspicious queries (PHTest).
limitations: (1) It never removes knowledge from the weights -- it is runtime detection + deflection wrapping a model that still contains the hazardous knowledge, unlike RMU/TAR which modify the model; the safety guarantee is only as strong as the Orchestrator/Evaluator agents, which are themselves LLM-prompted classifiers. (2) System-level overhead: each query passes through multiple LLM agent calls (orchestrate -> respond -> evaluate -> possibly deflect), a structural cost the paper does not quantify (no latency/throughput measurement). (3) Optimization needs a labeled validation set with ground-truth safe/unsafe annotations, so it presumes the defender can characterize the restricted-knowledge boundary in advance. (4) Reasoning models underperform: DeepSeek-R1 Distill-Llama-8B gives noticeably lower flagging accuracy than comparable non-reasoning models (authors speculate reasoning models work poorly with demonstration data and leave the cause to future work). (5) Evaluation is limited to the top-2 OWASP risks (jailbreak + info disclosure); broader threats like multi-turn injection or tool/agent misuse are not tested.

datasets_used: WMDP (unlearning of hazardous WMD-proxy knowledge; 3,668 MC questions across bio/cyber/chem)
PHTest (pseudo-harmful prompts for false-refusal / benign-compliance evaluation)
quantitative_results: WMDP Llama-3-8B (Table 2, AegisLLM): Cyber 24.4%, Bio 25.4%, Chem 27.2% accuracy (lower=better; 25% is random-guess floor); MMLU 58.4%; MT-Bench 7.57 -- lowest hazardous accuracy among all methods vs base 47.2/70.8/51.0% and MMLU 63.1 / MT-Bench 7.99
WMDP ablation Llama-3-8B (Table 5, optimized vs initial): accuracy Cyber 24.6% vs 31.7%, Bio 26.3% vs 32.0%, Chem 27.2% vs 35.8%; flagged ratio rose to 97.4/99.1/97.3% (from 67.1/87.6/67.4%)

TOFU abstention avg (Table 3): AegisLLM 97.10% vs Thaker baseline 59.43% (Llama-3-8B agents); Qwen2.5-72B 99.93% vs 98.67%; DeepSeek-R1 Distill-Llama-70B 97.33% vs 89.10%; DeepSeek-R1 Distill-Llama-8B 87.17% vs 79.43%
StrongREJECT (Table 4, Llama-3-8B): AegisLLM 0.038 vs base 0.078 (~51% reduction); competitive with but NOT best -- Self-Reminders 0.015, LLM-LAT 0.021, Circuit Breaker 0.022, Llama Guard 3 0.039
PHTest utility (Table 4): AegisLLM 88.5% compliance / 7.9% full refusal, vs LLM-LAT 39.2%/49.6%, Circuit Breaker 40.3%/50.9%, Self-Reminders 32.6%/55.7%, Llama Guard 3 80.2%/13.9%, base 85.8%/7.1%
Quick adaptation (Fig 4, StrongREJECT@Top15): 60.7% refusal at n=5 sample attacks -> 67.0% at n=10 -> 73.0% at n=15, with PHTest false-refusal only 8.7%/9.0%/10.3%
Data/compute efficiency: near-perfect WMDP unlearning from 20 training examples and fewer than 300 LM calls; optimization sample = 50 WMDP + 50 MMLU questions (~1.4% of WMDP)
baselines_compared: Unlearning: RMU, RMU-LAT, GradDiff-Merged, ELM-Merged, TAR, and Prompting/Filtering guardrails (Thaker et al.)
Jailbreaking: Circuit Breaker (Representation Rerouting), LLM-LAT Robust, Refusal-VPI, Llama Guard 3, Self-Examination, Self-Reminders
TOFU: post-processing LLM-abstention baseline (Thaker et al.)
